# Charter
In compliance with the newly approved City Ordinance No. 1, Series of 2025, which officially supersedes City Ordinance No. 7, Series of 2018, Gordon College affirms its full adherence to the updated directives as mandated by the local government. The institution remains committed to aligning its policies and operations with the latest regulatory framework to ensure a lawful, effective, and community-responsive higher educational institution.
[City-Ordinance-No.-01-s.-2025-An-Ordinance-Revising-the-Charter-of-Gordon-College](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/City-Ordinance-No.-01-s.-2025-An-Ordinance-Revising-the-Charter-of-Gordon-College.pdf)

# College Hymn
[![](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/play_button_gc-1.png)](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gc-hymn.mp4)
The sea breeze over Subic Bay  
Moderate waves to mighty rest  
Sets our heart in inspiring pace  
How Gordon College made us best  
Raise our voices in silence  
Sing the praises in cadence  
Let these color our daily life  
And withstand the pilgrimage.  
Bless our Alma Mater  
Keep her firm and strong  
And be able to mold us all  
Set us upright on the road  
Hear the echo of Her song  
Feel the warmth of Her voice  
Keep the flame of the Torch  
At Gordon College proudly we belong.

# College Seal
This is the official college seal/logo of Gordon College. This is to be used in legal and formal documents and correspondence of the College. The colors green and yellow signify the community college’s commitment to sustainable development or a development for all generations as it works towards environmental sustainability in its attainment of its vision, mission and goals. “Ulo ng Apo” represents the city of Olongapo where the local Higher Education Institution is based. This symbolizes the ability of its students to lead and make a difference in the community.
The torch illuminates a book which represents the ability to serve as a guiding light for others. It also shows how education is a beacon of light that nurture learning. It is strategically placed beneath the Ulo ng Apo signifying that knowledge must be used, not for knowledge’s sake, but more for the community which includes its stakeholders.
The circular wreath made of interlocking branches and leaves represents the unity and strength of stakeholders and its students who stand among the distinguished scholars the local HEI molded and developed. With the wreath of laurel leaves being a symbol of honor and victory, students, faculty and the leaders of the institution are expected to achieve victory over ignorance while remaining honorable and preserving integrity towards excellence.
The platinum colored shield placed behind the Ulo ng Apo represents the boldness of its students in pursuit of new horizons of unparalleled intellectual success. To remind its students staff and all the members of the institution to be bold and brave enough to explore and seek new opportunities beyond the four corners of the college towards the development of the community they belong to.
Under the shield is the year that Gordon College was founded. This represents the number of years of upholding its values. Three values guide the college, namely: excellence; character and service which shall guide the institutions’ trifocal function of instruction, research and extension.
The logo is in a form of circle, symbolizing the perfect fulfillment of a holistic educational process.

# History of Gordon College
Gordon College was an off-shoot of the Olongapo City Training Center, then a training center for skilled manpower for employment at the former Naval facility of the United States. With the facility’s closure, and the slowing down of demand for skilled workers, City residents shifted to professional courses. The unavailability of affordable tertiary institutions for urban poor sector of the population led the City Government of Olongapo, during the incumbency of Hon. Katherine H. Gordon, then City Mayor, to open the Olongapo City Colleges through City Ordinance no. 9 series of 1999.
Initially, the College offered Bachelor of Science in Accountancy and Bachelor of Science in Computer Studies to 177 enrollees. Additional four-year programs were offered in the succeeding years to accommodate the demand of City residents for other programs.
City Ordinance no. 42 series of 2002 was passed in May 2002, providing a change in the name of Olongapo City Colleges to Gordon College, its present name, in honor of the Gordon family who greatly contributed to the development of the City of Olongapo. In the same year, the College was officially accepted as a member of the Association of Local Colleges and Universities (ALCU).
In 2004, a revision on the legal basis for the creation and operation of Gordon College was made through City Ordinance no. 36 series of 2004. In the same year, the College was granted by the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Region III the certificate to operate with autonomy as per provisions of the Local Government Code of 19991. In addition to its undergraduate programs, the College opened its graduate programs in Education, Public Management and Business Administration.
In 2018, City Ordinance No. 07 series of 2018 was passed revising the charter of Gordon College subject to all Legal and Existing Rules and Regulations. This was superseded by City Ordinance No. 01 series of 2025 which was passed in 2025 that equipped Gordon College to expand its horizon for excellence to national and international level.

# Vision, Mission, Objectives, and Core Values
## Vision
A globally recognized local institution committed to innovative academic excellence, holistic and sustainable development, inclusivity, and community engagement.
## Mission
Produce empowered global citizens who create sustainable impact, uphold values of character, excellence, and service, and contribute to academic and societal development
## Strategic Objectives
1. **H**arness partnerships and linkages to produce graduates with global competencies aligned with the needs of the community and industry.
2. **O**rganize extension programs to sustain the greater impact of the institution to the community and environment.
3. **L**everage research outputs to improve existing knowledge and practices.
4. **I**ntegrate ethical principles and standards in the curriculum to strengthen the moral responsibilities of lifelong learners.
5. **S**upport continuous professional development and well-being of employees.
6. **T**ailor-fit student support services to ensure students thrive in the rapidly changing world.
7. **I**nvest in state-of-the-art facilities and technologies to enhance teaching, learning and research.
8. **C**ultivate a culture that embraces diversity and inclusivity.
## Core Values
* **Character.** Character is the foundation of a meaningful life. The institution strives to instill in students a strong moral compass, integrity, and a sense of responsibility for their actions. This includes fostering critical thinking skills, respect for diversity, and a commitment to a lifelong learning.
* **Excellence.** The institution provides a rigorous academic environment that challenges students to reach their full potential. Intellectual curiosity, innovation, and a pursuit of knowledge for its won sake is encouraged. It is believed that excellence is not just about achieving high grades, but also about developing the ability to analyze complex problems, think creatively, and communicate effectively.
* **Service.** Education empowers individuals to make a positive impact on their communities and the world. Students are encouraged to engage in service-learning opportunities that allow them to apply their knowledge and skills to real-world problems. The institution fosters a culture of social responsibility where students develop a sense of compassion and a desire to contribute to the betterment of society.

# Office of the College President
This office exercises overall responsibility and supervision over the College. The Chief Executive Officer of the College which is the College President is responsible for the articulation of the vision, mission, goals and objectives of the College and the development and implementation of educational programs and policies to achieve them. The office is directly accountable to the Board of Trustees for the organization, administration and supervision of the College.

# Continuing Students, Returnees and Cross Enrollment
## Continuing Students and Returnees
Continuing students are those who were previously enrolled in the College but unable to enroll for at least one semester and decided to continue studying in GC. These students have not requested for any transfer credential. They may have or have not filed for Leave of Absence.
Returning students are those who requested for transfer credentials but decided to re-enroll in the College. Returning students shall no longer be required to take the admission test.
## Cross Enrollment
* **GC Students to Enroll in Other Schools**
  + Students shall not be allowed to cross-enroll without proper consultation and consent from their respective College Deans. Students shall not be allowed to cross-enroll if the subject/course is not open during his/her last two semesters in the College. He/She shall request recommendation from his/her Dean and authorization coming from the College Registrar before he/she can cross-enroll to other colleges/universities.
* **Students from Other Schools to Cross-enroll in GC**
  + Students from other schools who would cross-enroll in GC should present a written permission from his school that he/she is allowed to enroll in GC.

# Freshmen Students
## Admission Requirements
Admission to Gordon College shall be open to Filipino citizens, both residents and non-residents of Olongapo City, Non-Filipino citizens, high school graduates, or passers of the high school equivalency certificate (PEPT), and shows evidence of being able to benefit from instruction. A NON-RESIDENT is defined as any individual who has lived less than one year in Olongapo City, is not registered to vote in Olongapo City and/or does not pay property taxes or contributes to the economic development of Olongapo City.
## New College Freshmen Students for Undergraduate Programs
* Admission to the chosen degree program will be based on an applicant’s Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT) results and previous high school/college academic records.
* Original copy of High School Report Card (F138/SF9) or Original ALS Certificate of Rating Secondary Level for ALS Passers
* Certificate of Good Moral Character
* Copy of Birth Certificate
* Medical Certificate (Issued by the Gordon College Clinic)
* Barangay Certificate of Residency
* One 2×2 picture

# IGS (Institute of Graduate Studies) Students
To qualify for admission to the Institute of Graduate Studies, an applicant must meet the following general and specific standards:
1. Student must have a minimum undergraduate GPA of 2.00 or 85% (except NSTP and PE). Student with lower general average than the prescribed rating may be admitted to the master’s program on probation. While on probationary status, s/he is allowed to carry the maximum load of only six (6) units per term. S/he will be placed in full standing status after having obtained an average rating of 1.5 or better in the first 12 units.
2. Specific programs may have different requirement as well as additional requirements such as materials and a personal interview, to wit:
   1. Master in Public Administration
      * At least two (2) years of government/ public service in any capacity
   2. Master in Business Management
      * BS Commerce or BS Business Administration graduate
      * Non-business course graduate must finish 18 tool courses. While earning these units, s/he may enroll any two basic/major subjects in the MBM program.
      * At least two (2) years of relevant experience/ service.
   3. Master of Arts in Education
      * Should be an education graduate
      * For non-education graduate, at least 24 units of professional education in the undergraduate level.
      * At least two (2) years of teaching experience/ service.
   4. Master of Arts in Nursing
      * Should be a Registered Nurse
      * At least two (2) years of experience in nursing and/or related field.
3. Pass the qualifying examination and interview.
4. All applicants for admission must submit the following;
   * Original Transcript of Records from recognized colleges or universities (with remarks, “Copy for Gordon College” or “Granted Honorable Dismissal”).
   * Two ID pictures (2×2/passport size)
   * Certificate of Employment
   * A Php200.00 non-refundable fee for application processing.
   * Additional materials, if required by specific program.

# Colleges
**Institute of Graduate Studies**
The mission of the Institute of Graduate Studies of Gordon College is to provide quality master’s degree programs and opportunities for continuing professional and career development for students whose academic preparation and personal characteristics foresee success in graduate studies. Each graduate program is firmly grounded in theoretical and empirical bodies of knowledge. Students are challenged to think critically and creatively, and to apply theories to practice in an atmosphere of inquiry and dynamic exchange with faculty and each other
**College of Allied Health Studies**
By 2025, the College of Allied Health Studies is envisioned to be a Center of Excellence in Allied Health Education in Region III and dedicated to being a forerunner in providing research-inclined instruction, evidence-based practice and community–responsive health care education committed to the holistic development of the human person.
**College of Business and Accountancy**
A center of excellence in accounting and business education that develops competitive leaders and professionals for providing professional training, innovative research and relevant extension services.
**College of Computer Studies**
The College of Computer Studies shall be the Center of Excellence in Information Technology and Computer Science for providing professional and technological training, scientific and innovative, and relevant community service
**College of Education, Arts and Sciences**
We envision the Gordon College-College of Education, Arts and Sciences to be a center of excellence in the field for providing professional training, innovative research and community service.
**College of Hospitality and Tourism Management**
To be a center of excellence in tourism and hospitality management with high standard through instruction, research and extension.

# Student Publication
**The Forefront** is the official student publication of Gordon College located in Olongapo City Sports Complex, Donor Street, East Tapinac, Olongapo City 2200. Its office is located at Rm. 516, 5th Floor, GC Main Building.
The Forefront was reestablished on June 2009, after a four-year hiatus, with the desire to promote the development and growth of responsible journalism as a means of strengthening ethical values, encouraging critical and creative thinking, and developing moral character and personal discipline in accordance with the Campus Journalism Act of 1991 (R.A. 7079).
**THE LOGO**
The official logo of the Forefront, as appeared above, is consist of two elements and two colors.
A *quill or pluma* refers to the writing profession. Its color, red, symbolizes courage and sacrifice. A *stem of laurel leaves* signifies triumph. Its color, golden yellow, symbolizes the nobility of the writing profession. The quill and the stem of laurel leaves form a capital letter “F” which stands for The Forefront.
**General Principles and Objectives**
The Forefront lives with its tagline, **“Informing and Empowering.”** To be able to uphold with its battlecry for campus press freedom, the Editorial Board sets the following General Principles:
1. The Forefront shall serve primarily as a newspaper of and for the students. It shall protect and promote the constitutionally mandated rights of students to free speech and of expression. In addition, it shall protect and promote the students’ rights and welfare.
2. The Forefront, being a publication of the students devoted to the ideals of truth, shall pursue the mission of social transformation through active and responsible journalism.
3. The Forefront, being an independent institution, shall not be subjected to any form of censorship. In turn, the editors and staff shall see to it that the Forefront exercises academic and campus press freedom in accordance with the laws of the Republic of the Philippines. It shall also seek to protect and promote  
   press freedom by taking firm and assertive stance against elements endangering such right.
4. The editors shall respect the rights of other parties that are equally protected by the laws of the Republic of the Philippines, affording them the right and opportunity to refute or reply to criticism or comment hurled at them.
Along with this principles, The Forefront shall at all times strive to:
1. Inform the public (i.e. the general population of the College, including the administration and the general public outside the College) with news of relevance and interest;
2. Serve as a venue for campus opinion and in maintaining an atmosphere of free and responsible discussion and of intellectual exploration;
3. Provide an outlet for students’ suggestions for the betterment of the campus;
4. Develop students’ powers of observation and discrimination concerning relative merits of news articles and;
5. Provide an opportunity for journalistic and creative writing.

# GC: Shortlisted for Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year (THE) Awards Asia 2025
[Image: GC: Shortlisted for Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year (THE) Awards Asia 2025]
[IPDEA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ipdea/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, April 24, 2025**
Gordon College is absolutely thrilled and incredibly proud to announce that we are one of the 8 finalists in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Awards Asia 2025 under the category of Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year! “This award recognizes the innovative use of digital technology to improve any function at a university, be that at institutional, departmental or faculty level”.
This incredible recognition is for our pioneering Gordon College Learners’ Academic Management Portal (GCLAMP), a testament to our commitment to innovative and accessible education for our students.
We extend our deepest gratitude and congratulations to the entire Gordon College community, especially to the brilliant minds behind GCLAMP. Our College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, our Vice President for Institutional Planning, Development and External Affairs, Ms. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, our Vice President for Administration and Finance and Project Leader of GCLAMP, Dr. Erlinda S. Casela-Abarintos, and our Coordinator of the International Affairs Unit, Ms. Precious S. De Castro, proudly represented Gordon College at the awards ceremony and the Asia Universities Summit in Macau, China from April 22-24, 2025.
A special kudos to everyone who has been part of the GCLAMP journey: the Commission on Higher Education Regional Office III (CHEDROIII) headed by Dr. Lora L. Yusi, our supportive City Mayor, Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., the Chairperson of the Committee on Education, Hon. Cristabelle C. Paulino, the dedicated GCLAMP development team, all our hardworking college personnel, and our valued stakeholders. Your collective efforts and support have been instrumental in this achievement!
This milestone reflects Gordon College’s dedication to pushing boundaries and embracing technology to enhance the learning experience. We are honored to be recognized among the leading institutions in Asia.

# 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025
[Image: 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, June 23, 2025**
Muling pinatunayan ng Central Luzon ang galing nito sa larangan ng edukasyon matapos mapasama ang 10 pampublikong Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) sa Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Ranking 2025.
Batay sa social media post ng Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Regional Office III, kabilang sa prestihiyosong listahan ang mga sumusunod na institusyon:
1. Central Luzon State University (801-1000)
2. Tarlac Agricultural University (1001-1500)
3. Bataan Peninsula State University (1501+)
4. Bulacan State University (1501+)
5. Don Honorio Ventura State University (1501+)
6. Nueva Ecija State University of Science and Technology (1501+)
7. Tarlac State University (1501+)
8. Gordon College (1501+)
9. Mabalacat City College (1501+)
10. City College of San Fernando, Pampanga (1501+)
Ang THE Impact Rankings ay sumusukat sa kontribusyon ng mga kolehiyo at unibersidad sa pag-abot ng United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), gaya ng dekalidad na edukasyon, gender equality, climate action, at iba pa.
Layunin nitong kilalanin at ipagdiwang ang mga institusyong aktibong nakikibahagi sa pagsusulong ng mas maayos at mas inklusibong mundo.
Ngayong taon, higit 2,000 universities mula sa 125 na bansa at teritoryo ang lumahok sa ranking. Sa bilang na ito, nasa 121 na institusyon sa Pilipinas ang kinilala, patunay ng lumlawak na global recognition pagdating sa kalidad ng edukasyon sa bansa.
Courtesy of CLTV36, click [here](https://cltv36.tv/10-heis-sa-central-luzon-pasok-sa-times-higher-education-impact-rankings-2025/?fbclid=IwY2xjawLhlMJleHRuA2FlbQIxMQABHjuiWKrpyze2X0ey_w9YqcxzFYqj-vVsA6VgMEYf5A0ksmU46n6TdywcL5ki_aem_Cu5pDIot_WbL-AJyV822ug) to read the full article.

# New Supreme Student Council proclaimed
[Image: New Supreme Student Council proclaimed]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [SSC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ssc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, May 6, 2025**
FRONTLINES | 𝑰𝒏 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒄𝒂𝒍𝒍 𝒕𝒐 𝒕𝒉𝒊𝒏𝒌 𝒘𝒊𝒔𝒆 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒗𝒐𝒕𝒆 𝒔𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒕, 𝒄𝒂𝒏 𝒂𝒏𝒚𝒐𝒏𝒆 𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒍𝒍𝒚 𝒔𝒂𝒚, ‘𝑰 𝒕𝒉𝒐𝒖𝒈𝒉𝒕 𝒘𝒊𝒔𝒆𝒍𝒚 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒗𝒐𝒕𝒆𝒅 𝒔𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒕’?
As the election results unfold, the following were proclaimed the new Supreme Student Council for A.Y. 2025-2026:
𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧: 𝐒𝐚𝐦𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐞, 𝐉𝐮𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐫
𝐕𝐢𝐜𝐞 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧: 𝐏𝐫𝐚𝐝𝐚𝐬, 𝐃𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐚 𝐃𝐞𝐥
𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐲: 𝐒𝐚𝐛𝐮𝐬𝐚𝐩, 𝐀𝐧𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐧𝐲 𝐀.
𝐓𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐫: 𝐃𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐝, 𝐏𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐜𝐞
𝐀𝐮𝐝𝐢𝐭𝐨𝐫: 𝐃𝐨𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐨, 𝐏𝐡𝐨𝐞𝐛𝐞 𝐀𝐧𝐧𝐞 𝐒.
𝐏𝐈𝐎: 𝐄𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐥𝐚, 𝐉𝐨𝐡𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐢 𝐄.
𝐂𝐀𝐇𝐒 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐔𝐦𝐚𝐝𝐡𝐚𝐲, 𝐑𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐫 𝐓𝐫𝐨𝐬
𝐂𝐁𝐀 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐚𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐧, 𝐍𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐀𝐧𝐧 𝐃.
𝐂𝐂𝐒 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐀𝐥𝐛𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐨, 𝐒𝐞𝐚𝐧 𝐃𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐝
𝐂𝐄𝐀𝐒 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐄𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐨, 𝐋𝐲𝐧𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐉𝐚𝐲 𝐏.
𝐂𝐇𝐓𝐌 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐘𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲𝐚𝐦𝐚, 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐘𝐨𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐤𝐨 𝐃.
As the students answer the call to vote wisely—which did not and will not end there—echoes collective raps to this new set of leaders about the accountability and responsibility of their respective positions.
Nothing much was asked by the students and for the students.

# GC produces 9 new CPAs; yields 39.13% pass rate in October CPALE
[Image: GC produces 9 new CPAs; yields 39.13% pass rate in October CPALE]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, November 4, 2025**
Nine alumni from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) of Gordon College (GC) were among the passers of the October Certified Public Accountant Licensure Exam (CPALE) per Professional Regulatory Board of Accountancy, on November 4.
GC garnered an overall passing rate of 39.13% from 9 passers out of 23 takers, surpassing the national passing rate of 34.02%. These constitute the 3,460 newly registered Certified Public Accountants (CPA).
Comprising this are three first-time takers and six repeater examinees.
In addition, Mike Ronnel Castro of Pampanga State University (for. Don Honorario Ventura State University) topped the examination with a 91.83% rating.
Bicol University – Daraga is the lone institution in the list of top-performing schools with 50 or more examinees with an 84.85% passing rate.
The licensure exam was conducted from October 24 to 26. | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting
[Image: BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, November 11, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE FUNCTION HALL MAIN BLDG—In an effort to strengthen transparency and communication between the student body and the administration, various students and representatives from different organizations voice out their concerns and proposed solutions on pressing campus issues that affect their daily experiences on the Town Hall Meeting, spearheaded by the Supreme Student Council (SSC) of Gordon College (GC) on November 4, at Gordon College Function Hall Main Bldg.
Among the major issues discussed were concerns with the disappearance of trash bins, restroom malfunctions, and the absence of student lounges, which reflect the everyday struggles of GCians that often go unnoticed. While these may seem like small matters, they collectively define the comfort, cleanliness, and morale of the student body.
Meanwhile, other pressing matters, such as the condition of the main and annex buildings and inconsistent implementation of the dress code, point to deeper issues of transparency, inclusivity, and student welfare.
DISAPPEARANCE OF TRASH BINS: CLEANLINESS AND WASTE MANAGEMENT ISSUES RAISED
Campus cleanliness had been a pressing issue among students following the recent disappearance of trash bins across campus. Students reported that many areas, including restrooms, now lack accessible waste bins, leading to increased littering and cleanliness issues.
Participants suggest restoration of the trash bins throughout the campus to promote cleanliness and for students’ easier access, emphasizing that this could help maintain a clean environment around the campus.
BUILDING FUNCTIONALITY: CALLS FOR BETTER FACILITIES BOTH IN THE MAIN AND ANNEX BUILDINGS
Moreover, the Annex Building, which accommodates a growing number of classes, points to another major concern. Students described poor ventilation, unstable internet connectivity, and a lack of proper equipment—including the sound system in the Function Room—as recurring problems that lessen their productivity.
Furthermore, students also expressed frustrations over the lack of equipment in the Main Building, such as practicums and simulations.
The SSC noted the importance of these concerns and emphasized the need for facility upgrades to ensure a more conducive academic environment, emphasizing that as Gordon College aims to be a university, infrastructure and facility improvements must keep pace with academic demands.
DRESS CODE VIOLATIONS: STUDENTS CALL FOR CONSISTENCY AND CLARITY
One of the most discussed topics was the implementation of the college’s dress code policy. Several students expressed confusion over inconsistent enforcement across departments, citing instances of inconsistency and a lack of clear communication throughout the student body.
“Minsan po navi-violation kami kahit appropriate po ‘yung suot namin [na uniform],” one Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Student expressed.
Participants also noted that some students are reprimanded for minor violations while others are not held to the same standard.
“Naka-org shirt po ako then ni-question po ako kung bakit [naka-org shirt]. Nag-explain po ako na may event, pero may nauna po sakin na naka-civilian [pero] ‘di po siya ni-question,” another student from College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) shared.
In the latter part of the meeting, Gcians requested clearer guidelines and a more uniform implementation across departments to help promote both discipline and inclusivity within the campus community, and avoid misunderstanding and unequal treatment.
STUDENT LOUNGE, RESTROOM, AND OTHER CONCERNS
The functionality and cleanliness of restrooms were also brought up as a long-standing concern. Some facilities were reported to have malfunctioning faucets and dysfunctional doors, toilets, and bidets. Students cite concerns for regular maintenance checks to ensure comfort and hygiene for all.
On the other hand, Participants also voiced out frustrations over the lack of student lounges and rest areas where they can relax, study, or collaborate during vacant periods. Many pointed out that the few available classrooms require permission to be used, forcing students to stay in hallways or stairwells in between classes.
One student from CHTM proposed designating student lounges on each floor to provide space for students as a resolution, citing that this would provide comfort for students.
The SSC assured attendees that all issues and recommendations raised will be compiled and formally addressed to the college administration for further deliberation with the BIBINGKA project, held on November 7, Friday.
The council also promised its commitment to continue facilitating such dialogues to ensure that students’ voices are not only heard but also acted upon. | via Love Lorraine Acedo/The FOREFRONT

# CCTV Privacy and Consent Notices
[Image: CCTV Privacy and Consent Notices]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/)
**Monday, January 2, 2023**
To all stakeholders of Gordon College, please be informed that the Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) cameras installed in the main building and premises of the college are used to process personal information, as defined by Republic Act 10173 or the Data Privacy act of 2012. These cameras capture and record the identities of individuals within their scope and are primarily intended for security and safety purposes. As mandated by the law, we would like to remind you that your consent is required for the processing of your personal information captured by these CCTV cameras. Your continued presence within the college premises is considered an implied consent to the processing of your personal information by the CCTV cameras. Rest assured that all personal information captured by the CCTV cameras will be treated with the utmost confidentiality and will only be accessed by authorized personnel for legitimate purposes. Thank you for your cooperation in ensuring compliance with the Data Privacy act of 2012.

# GC Ranks 3 in May 2025 PNLE; 2 Alumni Among Topnotchers
[Image: GC Ranks 3 in May 2025 PNLE; 2 Alumni Among Topnotchers]
[CAHS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cahs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, May 14, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) secures third spot among top performing schools in the May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE), earning a rating of 98.29%, as 115 out of 117 examinees successfully made the cut.
Moreover, two GC Alumni; Martin Jorge Bernal Alcantara and Loren Mirador Mistica, emerged as top 9, both acing a rate of 88.20%.
This milestone not only underscores the college’s commitment to academic excellence but also reinforces its standing as a premier institution for nursing education in the country.

# Stude orgs answer GC’s SOAE call

**Wednesday, February 26, 2025**
Despite the short attendance of student organizations and delegates in the GC 4th Student Organization Awards of Excellence (SOAE), the awarding ceremony proceeded in citing its outstanding student organizations and student-leaders in service for A.Y. 2024-2025 at GC Function Hall, yesterday.
The 4th SOAE awarded, in no particular order, its recipients of:
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization | Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP) – Gordon College Chapter, and Samahang Filipino (SamFil)
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization Adviser | Jehan B. Doblado, Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo, and Mychale Williams
Top 3 Outstanding Community Extension Program | CHTM Student Council, Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), and Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan
Meanwhile, Nicolas Milton Ching earned the Servant Student Leader of the Year while Klaudtrev Hanrick Payumo merited Rookie Student Servant of the Year. Jannae Mae De Jesus received the Student Organization Presidential Award and Sophia Joleen Evangelista with Outstanding Student Extensionist Award.
And in no particular order, this year’s Top 10 Outstanding Student Leaders were:
Nicolas Milton Ching | SPECS
Jhoemari Corpus | IONS
Janna Mae De Jesus | SMAP
La Arnie Morales | CHTM SC
Dhana Eunice Papa | JFINEX
Kharl Christopher Nofies | SAMFIL
Lorenzo Don Cayabyab | LTSP
Ralf Vincent Paule | PSCAS
John Kyle Perez | SAMFIL
Jay Angelo Manalang | LTSP
Aeros Raphael Morales | LELLS
Lynard Jay Ebalo | IONS
Mark Louis Macachor | CHTM SC
Sophia Joleen Evangelista | SMAP
Moreover, LTSP-GC Adviser Mychale Williams won the Student Organization Adviser of the Year.
The CHTM Student Council came out once more as the Student Council of the Year among others while IONS, after bagging two other awards earlier, also flexed on its Spirit of Service Award plaque while LTSP nagged the Student Organization of the Year title.
This ceremony is still in line with the celebration of the 26th Founding Anniversary of the institution.

# Proper Usage of Glass Boards
[Image: Proper Usage of Glass Boards]
**Thursday, May 23, 2024**
GUIDELINES FOR THE USE OF GLASS BOARD INSTALLED IN THE CLASSROOMS
* Use White Board Marker only to write on it
* Always erase marker ink from the surface after each use to prevent stains and ghosting.
* Don’t use dirty eraser or cloth, it will start spread ink residue across the surface of the board.
* Glass board must be cleared of any markings before the next class starts.
* Avoid using tapes or adhesives on the glass surface. These materials may be difficult and cause permanent damage to the glass board.
* Don’t use your hands to wipe a glass board! Your skin carries natural oils that over time leave a build-up of oil residue on the board. This can eventually ruin the surface of the glass board.
* Do not use sharp objects to avoid scratching or damaging the board surface.
* If the glass board is accidentally broken, it must be replaced with the same brand within seven (7) days from the date it was broken. If the item with specific brand and specification is not available, it must be replaced with three (3) timex of the quantity or in quantity comparable to the price of the item. The brand and specification must be approved first by the concerned personnel before replacement.

[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, August 17, 2025**
Gordon College is proud to announce that the Philippine Society of Customs Administration Students (PSCAS) Gordon College Chapter soared to new heights at the PSCAS National Awards on August 16, 2025, at Wind Residences, Tagaytay City!
PSCAS GC Chapter was honored with the most prestigious award: Top Performing PSCAS Chapter!
This incredible achievement is a testament to our dedication to excellence and unwavering commitment to our community.
We also brought home a constellation of awards, including Most Outstanding in:
\* Community Impact & Civic Engagement
\* Leadership & Institutional Development Projects
\* Specialized Customs Training & Webinar Programs
And we were recognized as Outstanding in:
\* Academic Excellence & Professional Development
\* Career Preparation & Skills Development
\* Culture, Arts & Wellness Promotion
\* Environmental Advocacy & Sustainability
\* Student Engagement & Community Building Initiatives
\* Student Support & Mentorship Initiatives
We want to give a special shout-out to two of our very own who stood out among the best students in the country:
\* Kris Rean Agaton was named the Top 1 Most Outstanding BSCA Student of the Philippines 2025.
\* Renmmelle Kyle Cerilles was named among the top 3 most outstanding BSCA Students in the Philippines in 2025.
Your hard work, leadership, and passion have made a mark, and we couldn’t be prouder of your achievements.
This incredible success belongs to every member, officer, and adviser who contributed their time and talent to make our chapter shine.
Thank you for your tireless efforts!

# World University Rankings 2026: GC Recognized as Reporter Institution
[Image: World University Rankings 2026: GC Recognized as Reporter Institution]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
Gordon College has officially been granted Reporter Status in the recent Times Higher Education (THE) World University Rankings 2026, released last October 9.
The college is among six Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Central Luzon that have been granted this recognition, including:  
– Bulacan State University  
– Central Luzon State University  
– Nueva Ecija University of Science and Technology  
– Tarlac Agricultural University, and  
– City College of San Fernando Pampanga
The Times Higher Education annually releases their World University Rankings that measure the performance of universities in eighteen (18) performance indicators across five core pillars: teaching, research environment, research quality, international outlook, and industry. A reporter status is granted to HEIs who have submitted relevant data but did not meet eligibility criteria to receive a rank. Inclusion in the reporter status allows HEIs to receive feedback in their performance across the metrics to support future decision making.
Meanwhile, Ateneo de Manila University is the highest ranking university in the Philippines with a banded rank of 1001-1200, followed by the University of the Philippines (1201-1500), De la Salle University (1501+), Mapua University (1501+). Mindanao State University – Iligan Institute of Technology (1501+), and University of Santo Tomas (1501+). The University of Oxford remains to be the top global ranking university for the tenth consecutive year. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT
Sources: Times Higher Education World University Rankings 2026 and CHED Regional Office III (via Facebook)

**Saturday, June 7, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) College of Business and Accountrancy (CBA) records 9 new Certified Public Accountant (CPA) in the recently held May 2025 Certified Public Accountant Licensure Exam (CPALE).
The institution aimed an overall perfomance of 56.25%, which surpassed the national rating of 33.11%, with a total of 9 out of 16 examinees joining 3,156 new CPAs in the country.
Out of 13 first time examinees, 8 passed the examination, with 2 considered as conditional. Additionally, among the 3 repeaters, 1 passed while another 1 was classified as conditional.
The licensure examination was administered by the Professional Regulation Comission (PRC) from May 25 to 27 nationwide.

# Gordon College Welcomes IYES Language School for Cultural Exchange and Nihongo Lesson
[Image: Gordon College Welcomes IYES Language School for Cultural Exchange and Nihongo Lesson]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, March 9, 2026**
Last March 6, 2026, Gordon College, through its International Affairs and Linkages Office, welcomed visitors from IYES Language School. Two Japanese students conducted an engaging educational and cultural exchange with 2nd Year BS Tourism Management students taking THP101 – Foreign Language 1 under Ms. Jacqueline Fabian, Instructor. The class visit featured a mini cultural presentation and an introductory Japanese (Nihongo) lesson, where the visiting students shared insights into Japanese culture, including common conversations and greetings, as well as interesting Japanese onomatopoeic expressions used in daily life. The highlight of the session was the interactive Q&A activity, where students enthusiastically exchanged questions and ideas, creating a lively and meaningful dialogue between cultures. This initiative aimed to foster cross-cultural understanding, provide opportunities for both visitors and students to share experiences and perspectives, and create a warm, interactive, and memorable learning experience for everyone involved.

# Empowering Solo Parents: Gordon College Launches ‘Weave of Strength’ Initiative
[Image: Empowering Solo Parents: Gordon College Launches ‘Weave of Strength’ Initiative]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, April 8, 2026**
Empowering communities through sustainable livelihood and inclusive development, Weave of Strength: Turning Scraps into Sustainable Livelihood was conducted on April 7, 2026 at the Gordon College Function Hall. The initiative provided solo parents and economically disadvantaged individuals with practical skills in transforming recycled fabric scraps into sustainable sources of income.
The program featured an inspiring message from College President Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano and the support of Vice President for RDCES, Dr. Ediric D. Gadia, reinforcing the college’s commitment to community service and development.
With the support of the Rotary Club of Downtown Olongapo, and through the coordinated efforts of RDCES and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management as the implementing unit, the initiative reflects Gordon College’s core values of Character, Excellence, and Service.
To ensure long-term sustainability, the initiative adopts a capacity-building approach by equipping administrative personnel to serve as future facilitators, enabling continuous implementation and expanded community reach.

# CHED validates the MAN program of Institute of Graduate Studies
[Image: CHED validates the MAN program of Institute of Graduate Studies]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, June 19, 2025**
The CHED Technical Panel for Nursing, Technical Evaluator for Nursing, and Regional Quality Assessment Team (RQAT) conducted onsite validation at Gordon College, Institute of Graduate Studies, last May 29-30, 2025.
The Gordon College welcomed evaluators from the Commission on Higher Education (CHED)—Regional Quality Assessment Team (RQAT) for an on-site validation aimed at securing a Certificate of Program Compliance (COPC) for the Master of Arts in Nursing (MAN) program.
Program Chair for MAN, Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr., introduced the CHED-RQAT evaluators: Dr. Maria Martina Geraldine Dimalibot, Dr. Al D. Biag, Dr. Mary Ann Gienette D. Medina, and Ms. Niña Jessa Angeli Palaganas.
The evaluators conducted thorough assessments of program administration, faculty credentials, curriculum, student services, facilities, and resources. Their findings and recommendations, discussed in a post-evaluation conference, will guide Gordon College, Institute of Graduate Studies continuing efforts to enhance academic quality.
Preparations were led by Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, Dean of the Institute of Graduate Studies; Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs; Dr. Narcisa R. Figuerres, Assistant Dean of the Institute of Graduate Studies; and Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr. Program Chair for MAN, Ms. Joan Joy A. De Vera; Executive Assistant of the Institute of Graduate Studies, Ms. Diana Grace M. Vallar; Executive Assistant of the Academic Affairs Office; and strong support from other college officials and faculty.
Gordon College, Institute of Graduate Studies, conveyed its sincere gratitude to the CHED-RQAT team for their invaluable insights and steadfast support, recognizing the visit as a major step in the institution’s ongoing journey toward academic excellence.

# Galaxies of Unity: CCS Students Explore New Worlds Together
[Image: Galaxies of Unity: CCS Students Explore New Worlds Together]
[CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [ForeFront](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/forefront/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 14, 2025**
ASINAN COVERED COURT, OLONGAPO CITY—The College of Computer Studies (CCS) of Gordon College launched its academic year with a universe of energy at the annual General Orientation and Acquaintance Party, “Galaxies of Unity: Exploring New Worlds Together,” held on September 13, 2025, at the Asinan Covered Court.
The event aims to transform into a constellation of colors, performances, advocacies, and camaraderie, welcoming both new and returning students into a community built on diversity, inclusion, and shared growth.
Furthermore, carrying the banner “Innovate, Include, Inspire: Pioneering Equality and Diversity in the AI-Driven World,” the orientation extended beyond the usual acquaintance party.
The event, spearheaded by Dean Ronnie Luy and Assistant Dean Armilyn Martinez with the support of the CCS faculty and coordinators, united students from the three CCS undergraduate programs—Bachelor of Science in Computer Science (BSCS), Bachelor of Science in Information Technology (BSIT), and Bachelor of Science in Entertainment and Multimedia Computing (BSEMC)—in celebrating inclusivity, teamwork, and talent.
“This event is not just for acquaintances, but also for orientation and presentation of our organizations and sportsfest participants. We want freshmen to feel welcome and informed, and for everyone to know the CCS community they belong to,” shared Ms. Yheng Miel Sanchez, CCS faculty.
At the heart of the program was the introduction of eight planetary teams, each representing a social cause: Lumora for Accessibility and Compassion (Persons with Disabilities), Veltraxis for Collaboration (Ethnicity and Race), Aurelia for Empathy (Senior Citizens), Cryonex for Innovation (AI and Robotics), Floraxis for Sustainability and Culture (Cultural Heritage), Kahelion for Resilience and Advocacy (Frontline Workers), Nyxara for Identity (LGBTQIA+), and Zenthara for Equity (Women Empowerment and Gender Equity).
Moreover, the day opened with an invocation, welcome remarks, and the general assembly before teams were formally revealed. Excitement built as each segment unfolded: cosplay presentations highlighted student creativity and confidence; production numbers filled the venue with music and dance; interactive games such as Constellation Connection, Bring Me, Inclusion Mosaic, One Word Explains All, Voices of the Galaxy, and The Matrix sparked laughter and teamwork; entrepreneurial booths offered handmade crochets, stickers, badges, and keychains; Echoes of Unity, the team chant presentations, raised school spirit; and raffle draws added surprise and fun throughout the day.
“Okay lang naman yung event, nakakapagod pero enjoyable. Pinakagusto ko yung cosplay, because makikita mo yung confidence and effort ng mga sumali,” said Jerry Andrew Famanila, CCS Student.
The cheers peaked with the introduction of players for CCS Sportsfest 2025, closing with the fiery chant, “Go CCS Phoenix!”. Student organizations, including the CCS Student Council, Society of Programming Enthusiasts in Computer Science (SPECS), Empowered League of Information Technology Education Students (ELITES), and Innovative Multimedia Artists Guild of Empowered Students (IMAGES), ensured the program’s smooth flow by managing venue setup, technical aspects, and coordination.
Between lively intermissions, the spotlight turned to academic achievements. The Dean’s Lister Awarding Ceremony honored the Top 10 achievers per program (BSCS, BSIT, BSEMC–Digital Animation, BSEMC–Game Development) and the Top 50 overall Dean’s Listers for A.Y. 2024–2025. Special recognitions, including Top Cosplayers, Best Production Number, and Winning Team Planet, added a celebratory flair, affirming that CCS nurtures both artistry and innovation.
In addition, John Derick De Leon, BSIT Student, shared that everything went according to the plan—which makes it very satisfying.
“Medyo mainit at may mga segment na biglaan pero doon lumalabas yung creativity ng students. Kitang-kita rin yung teamwork. Favorite ko yung part na bigla kaming pinag-drawing sa Inclusion Mosaic,” Mateo B. Bandilio, BSEMC student, shared.
Moreover, while the day brimmed with laughter, its deeper purpose was clear: to introduce freshmen to the CCS culture, strengthen bonds among students, and celebrate diversity as a source of strength. By merging advocacies with artistry and recognition with entertainment, the event became more than a kick-off celebration. It reaffirmed CCS’s mission to innovate, include, and inspire while promoting equality across differences.
As acknowledgments and final remarks drew the day to a close, ‘Galaxies of Unity’ stood as more than an acquaintance party. Guided by their advocacies, CCS students are set to carry the spirit of unity into the rest of the academic year, a reminder that while they may belong to different ‘planets’ of identity, talent, and cause, together they can form a universe of their own | Featured by Charyll Mae Cabungcal/The FOREFRONT

# BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers
[Image: BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, December 22, 2025**
The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) beams with pride as it yields 39 Licensed Professional Teachers (LPTs) for the Elementary level and 161 new LPTs for the Secondary level at this year’s Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT)
Gordon College (GC) once again surpassed the national passing rates in the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) conducted on September 21 and November 30, 2025.
For the Elementary Level, 39 out of 49 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, resulting in a passing rate of 79.59% against a national passing rate of 51.04%.
Meanwhile, for the Secondary Level, 161 out of 177 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, posting an overall passing rate of 90.96%, with 50% of second-takers also passing.
Overall, 76,696 out of 122,528 examinees nationwide passed the September–November 2025 BLEPT, representing a national passing rate of 65.04% for the secondary level.
Notably, the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) was rescheduled in Regions I, II and Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) due to inclement weather.
The examinations for the said regions were conducted on November 30. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# GC honors DASArving alumni, highlights iconic “first-times” in 4th DASA

**Tuesday, October 14, 2025**
SMX OLONGAPO CENTRAL— The 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Awards (DASA) 2025 gathered alumni who paved innovation and inspiration for Gordon College on October 11, held at SMX Olongapo Central.
Mr. Joseph Pusing, former Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI) President, proudly presented all the first-time projects that happened on the initiative of giving back to their alma mater: Alumni cards, Financial support for the student council and organizations, Cosme Y. Sering Library, and a fifty thousand cash donation for the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Department for the extension of rooms for accreditation.
“One of the highlights of our term is the donation of the alumni association’s newest student lounge, which was blessed on August 15, 2025. This will not be possible without the help and guidance of everyone,” he stated.
Also, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, the college president, exclaimed in her keynote speech that the world needs someone like them who wagers unselfish services in a world full of selfishness.
Furthermore, Ms. Rose Anne Cañeda Dumalo, alumna from the Bachelor of Science in Education – English (BSEd-English) Batch 2015, expressed in her speech that titles and recognition will not define us but the people that we serve along the way.
“Higit sa pagiging magaling ay ang pagiging mabuti. You may be the best in the field, but if you lack character, people will remember how you treated them more than how well you did your job,” she added.
Moreover, Ms. Ellinor T. Manaois-Yco, Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BSN) Batch 2011; Mr. Arben R. Cauilan, Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration (BSCA) Batch 2011; Nikki Antonette Chieng Carlos, Bachelor of Science in Tourism (BSTM); and Mr. Willy R. Antigo, Master of Arts in Education (MAEd) Batch 2019, were awarded with recognitions and plaques for their outstanding service and contributions.
In addition, Ms. Lovelyn P. Ceralde, DASA 2025 Overall Chairperson, extended her warm welcome and gratitude to the alumni, stakeholders, and those who have been bringing their best to provide a more quality education and experience inside the institution.
Finally, the program ended with an oath-taking for the newly elected GCAAI officers and board of trustees for the fiscal year 2025 – 2026. | via John Patrick Mateo/The FOREFRONT

# ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes
[Image: ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes]
**Monday, January 19, 2026**
**ANNOUNCEMENT:**
**ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes**
*Second Semester Academic Year 2025-2026*
**January 19-30, 2026**
[Image: ☑️]
Step 1
Log-on to the student portal ([https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student](https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExZWpzSlY3MHlQdVJWaXA0bnNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR5DCtJJZeE0IeMkAlOWYkAjlJ5HTa_uyuxL5M64lTq3dzJieHkPXJaQ0YalBg_aem_XfOZPmWrrx4p7jcYpDLqDg))
Click Add/Drop Request Button under Menu Items.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 2
To request for Dropping of classes, under Current Schedule section, click Drop button.
To request for adding of classes, under Add Subjects section, type the detail of the class (you can search by class code, subject or by description) on the search box. Click Add button of the desired class.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 3
Your selected subject(s) will appear under List of Subjects to Add/Drop section. Repeat step 2 to add or drop more classes.
Click submit to finalize your request or cancel to clear selected classes.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 4
Your request will be evaluated by your program coordinator and will be verified by the college registrar.
Regularly visit the GCES portal for request updates.

# Gordon College Joins National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill
[Image: Gordon College Joins National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, March 12, 2026**
LOOK | Students and personnel of Gordon College (GC) gathered earlier this afternoon, March 12, at Tapinac Oval Track as part of the National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill (NSED).
The earthquake drill simultaneously occurred at 3:30pm today in public and private institutions across the country to strengthen community resilience against a possible disaster.
NSED is practiced every quarter — totaling four times in a year — to establish nationwide earthquake preparedness. This is in preparation for possible earthquakes in the future including the “Big One”, a projected magnitude 7.2 earthquake predicted to hit Metro Manila and affect nearby provinces as the West Valley Fault is ripe for movement. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT

# Moving UP 2023
[Image: Moving UP 2023]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Wednesday, July 12, 2023**
The 13 sixth-grade graduates of Iram II Elementary School received a generous gift from the GC Community, represented by the GC CESU. The gift consisted of new unisex backpacks and essential school supplies like notebooks, ballpoint pens, paper, pencils, rulers, and erasers, preparing them for the upcoming academic year. Additionally, they were provided with a kilogram of rice, canned foods, noodles, soap, alcohol, and face masks, alleviating their household expenses for one to two days. These items were collected during the Moving-Up donation initiative, which saw enthusiastic participation from the entire GC community, including administrative staff, faculty, students, and student organizations.
Moreover, 19 kindergarten graduates were recognized for their dedication and hard work in their learning journey and were also presented with essential goods as tokens of appreciation.

# Artistry and Unity Take Center Stage at PASE Community Concert 2026
[Image: Artistry and Unity Take Center Stage at PASE Community Concert 2026]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
The PASE Community Concert 2026, held on the 2nd day of the 27th Founding Anniversary celebration and in observance of National Arts Month, became a memorable showcase of talent and creativity. The program featured performances from Pase Ballo, Pase Voci, Pase Teatro, and Pase Cuerdas.
The concert opened with dance performances from PASE Ballo, followed by a singing presentation from PASE Voci, and an acting performance from PASE Teatro, with each group having its own spotlight to showcase their artistry and skills. It marked the first time that all PASE groups performed together on one stage while highlighting their unique talents individually.
The presentation celebrated unity, creativity, and the passion for the arts, making the community concert a meaningful part of the anniversary and National Arts Month celebration.

# CHTM Sharks Roar at 2nd CHTM Rally 2025
[Image: CHTM Sharks Roar at 2nd CHTM Rally 2025]
[CHTM](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/chtm/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, September 11, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) filled the East Tapinac Covered Court with cheers and chants as the 2nd CHTM Rally unfolded last September 9, 2025, igniting the spirit of the CHTM Sharks in preparation for the much-anticipated Siklab 2025 Sportsfest on September 15–19, 2025.
The event showcased the final lineup of the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) official teams and players.
Furthermore, athletes from the CHTM Sharks introduced themselves through lively intermission numbers performed by each sports team, adding excitement and entertainment to the rally.
Player representatives from each team also participated in a brief interview with the Masters of Ceremony, where they shared their thoughts and enthusiasm for the upcoming Siklab competition.
Moreover, the event was organized in collaboration with the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management Student Council (CHTM-SC), Arapaap Productions, the Junior Association of Hospitality Management Students (JAHMS), and the League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP), who worked together to ensure the program’s success.
The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Dean, Dr. Mark Alvin Lazaro, Assistant Dean Vincent Dela Cruz, Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Program Coordinator Ma’am Lizette Ann P. Sia, Bachelor of Science in Hospitality Management (BSHM) Coordinator Vince Richard Gomez, and ARAPAAP Adviser Ms. Jenny Mae M. Lagman also extended strong support and leadership, which greatly guided the preparations for the rally.
To cap off the event, the atmosphere reached its peak as the CHTM Sharks filled the covered court with their department’s official Siklab cheer, embodying their spirit of unity and readiness for the upcoming sportsfest.| via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈
[Image: 𝐃𝐫. 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐮𝐢𝐨 𝐨𝐧 𝐆𝐂’𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐜 𝐋𝐚𝐛: 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐙𝐚𝐦𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬, 𝟏𝐬𝐭 𝐢𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐈𝐈𝐈]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [CCS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ccs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, May 22, 2025**
Gordon College (GC), through the College of Computer Studies (CCS), opened its Mac Laboratory on the 5th floor of the building, room 507, last April 28.
This new facility aims to equip students with the necessary competencies to keep up with the quick growth of technological advancements.
According to Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, the College President, the Mac Laboratory aims to innovate the school through integrating smart classrooms over the years and to, ideally, attract international students, preparing the students for the evolution of Gordon College into a university.
Meanwhile, in a previous report by The Forefront, students from CCS initiated the GC CCS Tech Fest as a fundraising event last February for the improvements of the Mac Lab room and some of its equipment.
When interviewed again, Kurt Jeehan Terado, one of the event’s heads, expressed his joy because their collective effort as students manifested in their school.
“Knowing that we were able to contribute something that future students will benefit from, I feel like everyone from our batch felt fulfilled,” said Terado.
To add to their efforts, the Mac Lab units came from the Local Government Unit (LGU) as the funds raised through this event are for the sole-purpose of room improvements.
Moreover, Dr. Darwin Paguio, Vice President of Academic Affairs, declared his pride for GC’s achievement.
“The first local college na magkaka-Mac laboratory. [The] first in zambales, [the] first in region 3,” Dr. Paguio stated.
With GC’s climb towards innovation, the institution also eyes international deployment of its students in Macau.

# Reading Literacy Training
[Image: Reading Literacy Training]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Saturday, June 10, 2023**
The GC CESU, Academic Affairs, and CEAS immersion instructors collaborated successfully to conduct a 3-day training for various CEAS academic programs. Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano played a pivotal role as the resource trainer, contributing to the training’s success. The training drew on the participation of a diverse group of student facilitators from ten programs within the College of Education, Arts, and Sciences. Specifically, 3rd-year students from BSEd (Mathematics, Science, English, Filipino, and Social Studies) and other programs like BPEd, BEEd, BCAEd, BECEd, and BAComm actively took part in the training. This training aligned with the goals of the MRP’s Reading Literacy Program for non-readers and out-of-school youth.

# GCian Alumni Makes Name on OSLP 2023
[Image: GCian Alumni Makes Name on OSLP 2023]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, October 11, 2023**
Mr. Marko L. Aratea and Mr. Nieki E. Garigo, both alumni from Gordon College’s College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS), stand out as finalists during the award ceremony for Outstanding Student Leaders of the Philippines (OSLP) 2023 held at Teacher’s Camp, Baguio City, on October 1, 2023.
In an interview with The Forefront, Aratea and Garigo claimed that the Office of Student Welfare and Service (OSWS) and GC-CEAS had announced that they would represent the institution in the aforementioned competition and directed all requirements to Transforming Advocates and Champions (TRAC).
“I was astonished and exhilarated when it was announced that I was one of the top 20 finalists. Knowing that out of 135 applicants across our nation and at various colleges and universities, I was chosen. For me, I consider that one of the great achievements that I had,” Aratea said, reflecting on how he felt when he knew that he was one of the finalists.
While Garigo stated, “Syempre napasigaw ako that time, because bago pa dumating sa point na yan ay marami kaming dinaanang proseso at screening. I still remember the day where they conducted our final interview and sakto pang nakaduty ako sa Cooperating School ko for my practice teaching, and as in, medyo mahina ang signal niyan kaya during my 1-hour interview, ay naroroon ako sa ilalim ng manga ng Regional Science High School para maging stable ang internet, and sobrang naging successful naman ng interview dahil every time na may mga ganyang screening, kasi ako I always put in my mind na parang “kachikahan” ko lang ang interviewer at sobrang ganda ng discussion namin, especially yung point na may SDG pa sa question, like I am not ready that time dahil ang nasa isip ko talaga niyan ang lesson sa susunod na subject na tuturuan ko. But thank God because everything went well.”
They expressed their deepest gratitude to TRAC for the opportunity they attained and to their co-finalists from different schools. The representatives would also like to thank Gordon College, the GC Alumni Association, Inc., GC-RDCES, GC-OSWS, GC-CEAS, their families, and lastly, the Almighty God.
“Be who you are, be true to yourself, and always know your advocacy by heart and mind. Think of this competition as a way for you to raise your voice as a student leader, an advocate, and a champion,” Aratea said, while Garigo stated, “Be a leader na hindi tuta; be a leader na boses ng mag-aaral; be a leader who is pro-people, pro-student, and pro-community,” as their advice for those aspiring student leaders of the institution.
Aratea was also hailed as the champion for the Student Research Paper Presentation at the said event.

**Thursday, August 17, 2023**
Brigada Eskwela served as a prime exemplar of how collaborative efforts across different sectors can enhance education in the Philippines. This program effectively united a diverse array of stakeholders to advance DepEd’s mission of ensuring equitable access to quality education for all Filipino learners.

# GCAT result, available within 3 days — ATU
[Image: GCAT result, available within 3 days — ATU]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, February 21, 2026**
OLONGAPO CITY, Philippines—Admission and Testing Unit (ATU) Coordinator Christine Moselina said on Monday, February 16, that the Gordon College Admission Test (GCAT) result will be available within three-working days after taking the exam.  
In an improvement over the previous week-long release period, the office has shortened the turnaround time of the admission test’s results to three days after examination.  
“Maximum na submission ng result [is] 3 days, ma-u-upload na sa [Gordon College Admission (GCA)] portal nila ‘yong kanilang result. Ang waiting game diyan is ‘yong evaluation [from] coordinator,” Moselina said.  
She also noted that the new system reverses the usual flow: applicants must provide their details and requirements, including their Grade 11 records instead of Grade 12 for initial review before they can sign up for a new GCA account.  
“Before, ang dami kasing nagkakaroon ng problems with regard to the submission ng requirements. [May mga] nakapag-register pero ‘yong requirements kulang-kulang, so kami ang nahihirapan sa admissions.”  
However, applicants are still required to provide their Grade 12 records to their coordinators for evaluation at the time of their interview.  
As of February 20, with a week remaining until the expected end, the office has logged 3,930 registrations, compared to the 5,666 total applicants recorded last year.  
“GCAT natin is until June 30 pa, matagal-tagal pa. Madadagdagan pa ‘yan,” the coordinator said.  
The admission unit opened early online applications for a three-month window, running from November 27 through February 27. | via Keane Rafael Abdon, THE FOREFRONT

# Responsibilities of the Data Subjects
Associated to the exercise of rights are responsibilities to respect the privacy of others and comply with Gordon College’s regulations in its administration of data privacy.
Data subjects shall have the following responsibilities:
1. Respect the data privacy rights of others;
2. Report any suspected security incident or personal data breach to Gordon College Data Protection Officer by sending an email or accomplishing OCP-DPU Form No. 4;
3. Provide Gordon College with true and accurate personal data and other information. Before submitting personal data of different people to Gordon College, obtain the consent of such people.
4. Not disclose to any unauthorized party any non-public confidential, sensitive or personal information obtained or learned in confidence from Gordon College; and
5. Abide by the policies, guidelines and rules of Gordon College on data privacy, information security, records management, research and ethical conduct. From time to time, check for and comply with updates on these policies, guidelines and rules. Students may also refer to their Student Handbook.

# LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon
[Image: LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon]
[LCUAA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/lcuaa/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, October 19, 2025**
The eagerly anticipated week-long 14th LCUAA National Games officially kicked off today at the City of San Jose del Monte (CSJDM) Sports Complex.
Cheers and applause filled the venue as the opening ceremony began with a parade featuring all 27 participating colleges, each led by their institution’s gold medalists.
Following the parade was the introduction of the LCUAA 2025 representative officers, a doxology by the Sining Tanglawan ng San Jose del Monte Performing Group, the singing of the National Anthem and the San Jose del Monte city hymn, and an energetic dance performance by the San Joseño World Class Talents troupe.
City College of San Jose del Monte (CCSJDM) College President Dr. Roger S. Ambida delivered the opening remarks, warmly welcoming delegates to the institution.
Hon. Arturo B. Robes then gave the keynote address, encouraging athletes to build a brighter and more empowered future.
Additionally, Dr. Lora L. Yusi, CHED Region 3 Director IV, gave an inspiring speech, highlighting the significance of LCUAA 2025 as a testament to the growth of local universities: “We see scholars who dream larger than themselves.”
The competitive events officially begin tomorrow, October 20, 2025.
Stay tuned to the official Facebook page of The Forefront for updates on the 14th LCUAA National Games. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara / The FOREFRONT

# Empowering GFPS members through the Application of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines
[Image: Empowering GFPS members through the Application of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, June 17, 2025**
The Gordon College, through its Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Unit as the GC-GFPS Secretariat, successfully facilitated a seminar for members of the Gordon College Gender and Development Focal Point System (GC-GFPS) entitled “Empowering GFPS Members through the Application of the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines” on June 13, 2025, held at the GC Library Audio-Visual Room.
This capacity-building initiative was organized to further strengthen the College’s gender mainstreaming efforts. It aimed to equip GC-GFPS members with the essential knowledge and practical skills needed to effectively integrate gender perspectives into the institution’s programs, projects, and activities. The seminar primarily focused on the application of the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG)—a critical framework designed to ensure that development initiatives are gender-responsive and aligned with the overarching goals of gender equality and women’s empowerment.

# GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year
[Image: GC Records 3,127 Freshmen for New Academic Year]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN CAMPUS —Gordon College (GC) experiences an increase in its total student population for Academic Year 2025–2026, highlighted by a significant surge in freshmen enrollment across all college departments.
According to the official report from the Office of the College Registrar, the total student population rose from 8,625 this Academic Year (A.Y.) 2025-2026, in contrast to the 7,505 recorded Gcians last 2024–2025.
Furthermore, the number of Level 1 students also recorded a drastic jump from 2,731 to 3,127 freshmen.
The College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) continues to lead as the largest department, with 2,431 total students and 767 freshmen for this academic year.
The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) recorded a population of 1,642 students and 676 freshmen.
The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) also experienced a notable increase in freshmen enrollees, from 468 last year to 663 this year.
While the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) saw an increase from 422 to 500 freshmen.
The College of Computer Studies (CCS) also recorded additional enrollees, welcoming 521 freshmen this year compared to the previous 442 record.
In line with its continuous growth, the CBA has officially moved into its new home at the GC Annex Building in Kalaklan, marking a new chapter for the college. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

# GC holds mental health seminar, boosts self-harm awareness among students
[Image: GC holds mental health seminar, boosts self-harm awareness among students]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, March 23, 2026**
To raise awareness on self-harm, the Gordon College – Office of Student Welfare and Services (GC-OSWS), in collaboration with GC – Guidance Counseling Unit (GC-GCU), conducted the “Nature of Self-Harm: Awareness, Compassion, and Prevention” seminar last March 13 at the Function Hall, GC – Main Building.
Graced by Resource Speaker Erin RiegodeDios Asio, a Master of Arts in Education Major in Behavioral Sciences graduate, the seminar featured how Non-Suicidal Self-Injury (NSSI), also known as self-harm, is different from suicide.
According to Asio, self-harm is a way of coping intense feelings by hurting oneself on purpose. The intention of doing self-harm is to feel alive and escape emotional pain.
Additionally, she stated that self-harm used to be listed as a symptom of Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD), a pervasive disorder that affects the whole personality. Most people who self-harm do not have BPD, but people who have BPD may have these tendencies.
The seminar went further and discussed the different types of self-harm: cutting, burning, and hitting. If NSSI is done occasionally, there is a risk of making an unintentional mistake that could lead to accidental death.
Asio explained the phases of taking action when someone is afflicted with self-harm. Where the first phase is listening to someone when they open up about hurting themselves. Then, offer support toward counseling.
Then followed by the causes, myths, and how to cope with self-harm in a healthy way. She also told students that they should check up on each other afterwards.
The keynote speaker also underscored the importance of promoting a stigma-free, healthy, and supportive environment within the school.
Students are welcome at the GCU office for free counseling services to those struggling with their mental health. | via Jasmine Zeah Llagas/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College GAD Focal Point System Conducts Benchmarking Visit at PLM
[Image: Gordon College GAD Focal Point System Conducts Benchmarking Visit at PLM]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 11, 2025**
Representatives from various departments of Gordon College, serving as members of the Gender and Development (GAD) Focal Point System, successfully concluded a benchmarking visit to Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Maynila (PLM) last August 08, 2025. The activity aimed to strengthen the College’s GAD programs by learning from PLM’s best practices in promoting gender equality, inclusivity, and institutional development.
The delegation was warmly welcomed by PLM officials and staff. The visit featured comprehensive presentations on PLM’s Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) framework, gender-responsive policies, and student-centered initiatives. Delegates also toured PLM’s academic and administrative facilities, including specialized units dedicated to personnel and student support and welfare.
An open forum provided an opportunity for Gordon College representatives from different units to engage with PLM counterparts, exchanging strategies on implementing GAD-related projects, integrating gender perspectives in policies.
This activity reinforces Gordon College’s commitment to fostering a gender-responsive, inclusive, and equitable academic environment through continuous learning and partnership with other higher education institutions.
